title: Cat Scratching Ears Until They Bleed: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat scratching their ears until they bleed? Learn about potential causes, urgency levels, and how to help your feline friend find relief.
content_html:
What Is Causing My Cat to Scratch Their Ears?

When a cat scratches their ears to the point of bleeding, it is usually a sign of intense itching, known as pruritus. This behavior is most commonly triggered by ear mites, which are microscopic parasites that cause extreme irritation. Other frequent causes include bacterial or yeast infections, flea allergies, or environmental allergies to pollen and dust. The bleeding occurs because the skin on the ear flap is very thin and easily damaged by sharp claws.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While bleeding from scratches is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a sign of significant distress and pain for your cat. If left untreated, the constant trauma can lead to an aural hematoma (a large blood-filled swelling in the ear flap) or severe secondary infections. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours to address the underlying cause and provide pain relief.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, close-up photo of the ear can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. High-quality images allow a professional to see the type of discharge present—such as the dark, crumbly debris associated with mites versus yellow pus from an infection. Seeing the extent of the skin damage also helps the triage team determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and whether a specialized ear exam is required.

faqs.question: Can I use human ear drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter drops without professional guidance, as some ingredients can be toxic to cats or cause permanent damage if the eardrum is ruptured.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: You can apply gentle, firm pressure with a clean cloth or gauze for 5 minutes. If the bleeding persists, contact a vet immediately. Avoid using 'stypic powder' inside the ear canal.

faqs.question: What are common signs of an ear infection in cats?
faqs.answer: Beyond scratching, look for redness, a foul odor, discharge, frequent head shaking, or your cat tilting their head to one side.
